metadata.summary: Test multiple components against real dependencies (databases, APIs, services) using isolation strategies, FastAPI/Django patterns, and respx/WireMock mocking.

content.title: Integration Testing
content.summary: Integration tests catch the class of bugs that unit tests cannot: ORM query bugs, constraint violations, serialization mismatches between layers, and authentication middleware failures. This methodology covers test isolation strategies, database patterns with Testcontainers and pytest-django, FastAPI/Django API testing with dependency injection, and external service mocking with respx and WireMock.

why.p: Integration tests catch the class of bugs that unit tests cannot: ORM query bugs, constraint violations, serialization mismatches between layers, and authentication middleware failures. Without them, these bugs reach production.

list.item: API endpoints with real request/response validation
Testing a single pure function with no external calls — that is a unit test
Testing a full user journey through the browser — that is E2E

section.name: test-isolation-strategies
section.p: Four strategies for preventing integration tests from interfering with each other: transaction rollback (fastest), table truncate (accurate), Testcontainers (reproducible), and unique IDs (parallelizable). Choose based on the accuracy vs. speed trade-off.

list.item: Transaction rollback — fastest; wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back; misses commit-time errors (unique constraints)
Truncate tables — medium speed; catches all errors; accurate but slower than rollback
Testcontainers — slowest startup, fastest per-test after warm-up; real DB behavior; requires Docker; ideal for CI
Unique IDs per test run — allows parallel execution; data accumulates; suitable when cleanup is handled separately

section.note: Combine strategies: session-scoped Testcontainers container (one Docker start) + function-scoped transaction rollback (fast per-test isolation). This is the recommended default.

rule.statement: Use transaction rollback as the default isolation strategy: begin a transaction in the fixture, yield the session, then rollback unconditionally in teardown.
rule.rationale: Rollback is faster than DELETE or TRUNCATE because it is a single low-level operation. The test never commits, so the database state is always clean for the next test.

example.title: Transaction rollback fixture

rule.statement: When running tests in parallel, use UUID-based unique identifiers for email addresses, usernames, and any unique-constrained fields instead of relying on cleanup.
rule.rationale: Parallel test workers cannot share a transaction; unique IDs prevent constraint violations without serializing execution.

example.title: UUID-based unique email fixture

rule.statement: Scope the Docker container fixture to the session and the transaction session fixture to the function; this starts Docker once and gets per-test rollback isolation.
rule.rationale: Session scope for the container avoids Docker startup cost on every test. Function scope for the DB session ensures each test starts with a clean slate via rollback.

example.title: PostgreSQL Testcontainers with SQLAlchemy

rule.statement: Use @pytest.mark.django_db for standard tests with automatic rollback; use transaction=True only when the test must verify commit-time behavior (e.g., unique constraint errors).
rule.rationale: transaction=True wraps the test in a real transaction that commits, which is slower and requires explicit cleanup. The default wraps in a savepoint that rolls back.

example.title: pytest-django with transaction behavior testing

section.name: factory-boy

rule.statement: Define a DjangoModelFactory per model; use factory.Sequence for unique fields; use factory.Faker for realistic but arbitrary data; expose named fixtures per common test scenario.
rule.rationale: Factories generate valid objects with minimal boilerplate. Sequences guarantee uniqueness without UUID overhead. Named fixtures (user, admin_user) communicate intent better than raw factory calls.

example.title: Factory Boy with Django

section.reference: Full Django conftest with Factory Boy

rule.statement: Override the get_db dependency with a fixture that yields the test session; clear overrides in teardown; never call the app directly without the override — the test will use the production database.
rule.rationale: dependency_overrides is FastAPI's injection replacement mechanism. Without clearing it after the test, overrides leak into subsequent tests.

example.title: FastAPI TestClient with dependency override

section.name: fastapi-asyncclient-async

rule.statement: Use httpx.AsyncClient with ASGITransport for async endpoint tests; mark tests with @pytest.mark.anyio; override dependencies the same way as synchronous tests.
rule.rationale: TestClient runs the ASGI app in a thread. AsyncClient drives it in the same event loop, which is required when the application code uses async database drivers (asyncpg, motor) that cannot be called from a thread.

example.title: FastAPI AsyncClient for async endpoints

section.reference: Full FastAPI sync + async client fixtures

rule.statement: Use @respx.mock to intercept outbound httpx requests in tests; define route → response mappings before calling the code under test; assert call counts and request bodies after.
rule.rationale: respx operates at the transport layer, intercepting real httpx calls without any code changes. This is the minimal-overhead way to mock HTTP dependencies in Python async code.

example.title: respx mocking external API calls

rule.statement: Use a WireMock container (session-scoped) when the code under test calls multiple endpoints, uses HTTP features (redirects, cookies, chunked transfer), or when you need realistic HTTP behavior that respx cannot simulate.
rule.rationale: WireMock runs a real HTTP server; the application connects to it exactly as it would to the real service. This catches connection pooling bugs, header parsing issues, and retry logic that mock transports miss.

example.title: WireMock via Testcontainers for payment flow

antipattern.description: Testing against a production database or live external API in CI.
antipattern.reason: Network failures and data mutations cause flaky tests and real side effects. Use Testcontainers for databases and respx/WireMock for HTTP dependencies.

antipattern.description: Mocking the integration layer to test integration behavior.
antipattern.reason: If you mock the database in an "integration test", you are writing a unit test. Integration tests must use real (or containerized) services to validate the actual connection.

antipattern.description: Sharing mutable test data across integration tests without rollback or cleanup.
antipattern.reason: Tests become order-dependent. One test's inserted row can cause another test's unique constraint violation, creating intermittent failures that are hard to reproduce.

template.purpose: Session-scoped Postgres container + function-scoped transaction rollback session
Django conftest with Factory Boy UserFactory and admin_user fixture
FastAPI TestClient and AsyncClient fixtures with dependency override
